title: Conditioning a leather armchair
subtitle: Clean and feed a leather sofa or armchair so the surface stays soft and the colour holds
excerpt: How to clean a leather armchair, work in a conditioner, and buff to a soft sheen. Covers identifying the leather type, picking the right cleaner and conditioner, and the twice-yearly cadence that keeps a leather seat in service for decades.
sourceNotes: Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Leather Conservation Centre care guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leather coloured with a dye only, with no surface coating. Soft, warm, and shows grain and age clearly. Stains and water-marks easily and needs conditioning twice a year.

Leather coated with a thin pigmented finish layer over the dyed surface. Harder-wearing, less prone to staining, and the most common modern furniture leather. Conditioning works on the coating rather than the leather itself.

content.text: A leather armchair lasts 30 years if it is cleaned and conditioned twice a year. The job takes an hour per chair. The first step is to know whether the leather is
aniline
or
pigmented
; the conditioner choice depends on it.
Step 1, identify the leather

Put a single drop of water on a hidden spot at the back of the chair. If the drop soaks in within a minute, the leather is aniline. If the drop sits on the surface as a bead, the leather is pigmented. This determines which conditioner is right; an aniline conditioner on pigmented leather sits as a sticky film, and a pigmented conditioner on aniline soaks in too fast to do any good.
Step 2, vacuum and dust

Run the vacuum over the chair with a soft brush attachment. Get into the folds at the cushion edge, the gap between the seat and the back, and the seam lines on the arms. Dust grit caught in the leather scratches the surface during cleaning if it is not lifted first.
Step 3, wipe with leather cleaner

Apply a small amount of leather cleaner to a soft clean cloth. Wipe a small area in light circles. The cloth picks up grey or brown dirt. Turn the cloth often. Work across the whole chair section by section. Do not soak the leather. Avoid household cleaners; even mild detergents strip the leather of natural oils.
Step 4, allow to dry
Leave the leather to dry naturally for an hour. Do not use a hair dryer or place the chair near a radiator. Heat dries the leather too quickly and causes cracking.
Step 5, work in the conditioner

Squeeze a pea-sized amount of leather conditioner onto a fresh cloth. Rub into the leather in small circles. The conditioner darkens the surface as it is worked in; the colour returns as it dries. Work each section in turn. Use a fingertip on a third cloth to push the conditioner into the seam lines and cushion edges.
Step 6, leave to absorb and buff
Leave the chair to absorb the conditioner for two hours. Buff with a clean soft cloth in long strokes to bring up a soft sheen. The chair is ready for normal use.
Variations

On a Chesterfield with a deep aged patina, skip the cleaner step entirely. Dust only, then condition. The patina is the value; cleaning takes it back to a younger look. On a heavily soiled pigmented chair, a saddle soap step before the leather cleaner shifts the surface dirt; saddle soap is too strong for aniline.
Care

Condition twice a year. Wipe spills with a dry cloth at once. Keep the chair out of direct sunlight; leather fades in two summers next to a south-facing window. A weekly dust with a dry cloth keeps the surface clean between full conditioning sessions.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The conditioner left a sticky surface that does not dry
attrs.cause: Wrong conditioner for the leather type, or too much applied in one pass.
attrs.fix: Wipe off the excess with a clean dry cloth. Allow 24 hours. If the surface is still sticky, wipe with a damp cloth and re-identify the leather type.

attrs.symptom: The leather feels dry and stiff after conditioning
attrs.cause: Too little conditioner applied, or the leather was already past the point a single session can recover.
attrs.fix: Repeat the conditioner step every week for three weeks, then return to the twice-yearly schedule. Old neglected leather takes a few sessions to come back.

attrs.symptom: The colour rubbed off on the cloth during cleaning
attrs.cause: Aniline leather releases dye when wet; a small amount of colour transfer is normal. Heavy colour transfer means a damaged dye coat.
attrs.fix: Stop cleaning. Test in a hidden spot to confirm. If colour transfer continues, the chair needs a leather dye refresh, not a general clean.
